---
title: "quiz10"
title-block-style: none
format: live-html
engine: knitr
sidebar:
  style: docked
resources:
  -  marketing_A.csv
  -  marketing_B.csv
---

{{< include /_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}
{{< include /_extensions/r-wasm/live/_gradethis.qmd >}}

<script>
document.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", () => {
  const params = new URLSearchParams(window.location.search);
  const showQ = params.get("q");

  // Show only the requested section
  if (showQ) {
    document.querySelectorAll("section[id]").forEach(section => {
      if (section.id !== showQ) section.style.display = "none";
    });
  }

  // Hide page title
  document.querySelectorAll(".quarto-title-block")
    .forEach(el => el.style.display = "none");

  // Hide ALL section titles (## ...)
  document.querySelectorAll("section[id] > h2")
    .forEach(h => h.style.display = "none");
});
</script>

## Q1 — Visualize the Relationship {#q1a}
Using the `marketing_A.csv` dataset:

Does the plot suggest a linear relationship?
```{webr}
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_A.csv") 
plot(quiz$ad_spend, quiz$sales,
     xlab = "Ad Spend ($)", ylab = "Sales ($)")
abline(lm(sales ~ ad_spend, data = quiz), col = "blue")
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false

# Yes
```

## Q1 — Visualize the Relationship {#q1b}
Using the `marketing_B.csv` dataset:

Does the plot suggest a linear relationship?
```{webr}
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_B.csv") 
plot(quiz$ad_spend, quiz$sales,
     xlab = "Ad Spend ($)", ylab = "Sales ($)")
abline(lm(sales ~ ad_spend, data = quiz), col = "blue")
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false

# Yes
```

## Q2 — Fit the Regression Model {#q2a}

Using the `marketing_A.csv` dataset:

Fit a simple linear regression model with `sales` as the response and `ad_spend` as the predictor.

Return the estimated slope.

```{webr}
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_A.csv")
model <- lm(___ ~ ___, data = quiz)
coef(model)
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_A.csv")
model <- lm(sales ~ ad_spend, data = quiz)
coef(model)
# 2.1472
```

## Q2 — Fit the Regression Model {#q2b}

Using the `marketing_B.csv` dataset:

Fit a simple linear regression model with `sales` as the response and `ad_spend` as the predictor.

Return the estimated slope.

```{webr}
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_B.csv")
model <- lm(___ ~ ___, data = quiz)
coef(model)
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_B.csv")
model <- lm(sales ~ ad_spend, data = quiz)
coef(model)
# 3.4588
```
## Q3 — Compute R² {#q3a}

Using the `marketing_A.csv` dataset:

Calculate the coefficient of determination $R^2$.
```{webr}
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_A.csv")
model <- lm(___ ~ ___, data = quiz)
summary(model)
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_A.csv")
model <- lm(sales ~ ad_spend, data = quiz)
summary(model)
# 0.1463 
```
## Q3 — Compute R² {#q3b}

Using the `marketing_B.csv` dataset:

Calculate the coefficient of determination $R^2$.
```{webr}
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_B.csv")
model <- lm(___ ~ ___, data = quiz)
summary(model)
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_B.csv")
model <- lm(sales ~ ad_spend, data = quiz)
summary(model)
# 0.2339
```
## Q4 — Compute the p-value for the slope {#q4a}

Using the `marketing_A.csv` dataset:

Compute the p-value for testing $H_0: \beta_1 = 0$, where $\beta_1$ is the slope coefficient for `ad_spend`.

 is the slope coefficient for ad_spend.

```{webr}
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_A.csv")
model <- lm(___ ~ ___, data = quiz)
summary(model)
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_A.csv")
model <- lm(sales ~ ad_spend, data = quiz)
summary(model)
# 0.0370
```

## Q4 — Compute the p-value for the slope {#q4b}

Using the `marketing_B.csv` dataset:

Compute the p-value for testing $H_0: \beta_1 = 0$, where $\beta_1$ is the slope coefficient for `ad_spend`.

```{webr}
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_B.csv")
model <- lm(___ ~ ___, data = quiz)
summary(model)
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_B.csv")
model <- lm(sales ~ ad_spend, data = quiz)
summary(model)
# 0.0068
```

## Q5 - Interpret R-squared {#q5a}

Using the `marketing_A.csv` dataset:

Which of the following best interprets the coefficient of determination (R-squared) from the fitted regression model?

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
# A. About R-squared% of the variation in ad spend is explained by sales
# B. About R-squared% of the variation in sales is explained by ad spend
# C. About R-squared% of the variation in sales is caused by ad spend
# D. About R-squared% of the variation in ad spend is caused by sales

# Answer: B. About R-squared% of the variation in sales is explained by ad spend
```

## Q5 - Interpret R-squared {#q5b}

Using the `marketing_B.csv` dataset:

Which of the following best interprets the coefficient of determination (R-squared) from the fitted regression model?

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
# A. About R-squared% of the variation in ad spend is explained by sales
# B. About R-squared% of the variation in sales is explained by ad spend
# C. About R-squared% of the variation in sales is caused by ad spend
# D. About R-squared% of the variation in ad spend is caused by sales

# Answer: B. About R-squared% of the variation in sales is explained by ad spend
```

## Q6 - Correlation Coefficient R {#q6a}

Using the `marketing_A.csv` dataset:

Calculate the correlation coefficient ($R$) between ad spend and sales.

```{webr}
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_A.csv")
cor(___, ___)
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_A.csv")
cor(quiz$ad_spend, quiz$sales)
# 0.3825
```

## Q6 - Correlation Coefficient R {#q6b}

Using the `marketing_B.csv` dataset:

Calculate the correlation coefficient ($R$) between ad spend and sales.

```{webr}
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_B.csv")
cor(___, ___)
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_B.csv")
cor(quiz$ad_spend, quiz$sales)
# 0.4836
```

## Q7 — Interpret the correlation coefficient {#q7}
(On Quercus directly)
Question:

Using your answer from Q6, which of the following best describes the correlation coefficient (R) between ad spend and sales?

Options:

A. Strong positive correlation
B. Weak positive correlation
C. Strong negative correlation
D. Weak negative correlation

Answer: B. Weak positive correlation

## Q8 — Prediction (Interpolation) {#q8a}

Using the `marketing_A.csv` dataset:

Predict the mean `sales` when `ad_spend` = 6000.

(*Round your answers to 2 decimal places.*)
```{webr}
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_A.csv")
model <- lm(___ ~ ___, data = quiz)
predict(___, newdata = data.frame(ad_spend = ___))
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_A.csv")
model <- lm(sales ~ ad_spend, data = quiz)
predict(model, newdata = data.frame(ad_spend = 6000))
# 26963.53 
```

## Q8 — Prediction (Interpolation) {#q8b}

Using the `marketing_B.csv` dataset:

Predict the mean `sales` when `ad_spend` = 6000.

(*Round your answers to 2 decimal places.*)
```{webr}
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_B.csv")
model <- lm(___ ~ ___, data = quiz)
predict(___, newdata = data.frame(ad_spend = ___))
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_B.csv")
model <- lm(sales ~ ad_spend, data = quiz)
predict(model, newdata = data.frame(ad_spend = 6000))
# 28484.35
```

##  Q9 — Interpolation Reliability {#q9a}
For the prediction at `ad_spend = 6000`, which of the following is the best conclusion based on the observed data?
```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false

# A. Yes, the prediction is reliable because we are conducting interpolation.
# B. Yes, the prediction is reliable because we are conducting extrapolation.
# C. No, the prediction is not reliable because we are conducting interpolation.
# D. No, the prediction is not reliable because we are conducting extrapolation.

# Answer: A. Yes, the prediction is reliable because we are conducting interpolation.
```

##  Q9 — Interpolation Reliability {#q9b}
For the prediction at `ad_spend = 6000`, which of the following is the best conclusion based on the observed data?

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false

# A. Yes, the prediction is reliable because we are conducting interpolation.
# B. Yes, the prediction is reliable because we are conducting extrapolation.
# C. No, the prediction is not reliable because we are conducting interpolation.
# D. No, the prediction is not reliable because we are conducting extrapolation.

# Answer: A. Yes, the prediction is reliable because we are conducting interpolation.
```
## Q10 — Prediction (Extrapolation) {#q10a}

Using the `marketing_A.csv` dataset:

Predict the mean `sales` when `ad_spend` = 20000.

(*Round your answers to 2 decimal places.*)
```{webr}
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_A.csv")
model <- lm(___ ~ ___, data = quiz)
predict(___, newdata = data.frame(ad_spend = ___))
```


```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_A.csv")

model <- lm(sales ~ ad_spend, data = quiz)
predict(model, newdata = data.frame(ad_spend = 20000))
#57024.26
```

## Q10 — Prediction (Extrapolation) {#q10b}

Using the `marketing_B.csv` dataset:

Predict the mean `sales` when `ad_spend` = 20000.

(*Round your answers to 2 decimal places.*)
```{webr}
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_B.csv")
model <- lm(___ ~ ___, data = quiz)
predict(___, newdata = data.frame(ad_spend = ___))
```


```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_B.csv")

model <- lm(sales ~ ad_spend, data = quiz)
predict(model, newdata = data.frame(ad_spend = 20000))
#76907.01
```


## Q11 — Extrapolation Reliability {#q11a}

For the prediction at `ad_spend = 20000`, which of the following is the best conclusion based on the observed data?

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false

# A. Yes, the prediction is reliable because we are conducting interpolation.
# B. Yes, the prediction is reliable because we are conducting extrapolation.
# C. No, the prediction is not reliable because we are conducting interpolation.
# D. No, the prediction is not reliable because we are conducting extrapolation.

# Answer: D. No, the prediction is not reliable because we are conducting extrapolation.

```

## Q11 — Extrapolation Reliability {#q11b}

For the prediction at `ad_spend = 20000`, which of the following is the best conclusion based on the observed data?

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false

# A. Yes, the prediction is reliable because we are conducting interpolation.
# B. Yes, the prediction is reliable because we are conducting extrapolation.
# C. No, the prediction is not reliable because we are conducting interpolation.
# D. No, the prediction is not reliable because we are conducting extrapolation.

# Answer: D. No, the prediction is not reliable because we are conducting extrapolation.


```

## Q12 — Residual Plot {#q12a}

Using the `marketing_A.csv` dataset, fit a simple linear regression model with `sales` as the response and `ad_spend` as the predictor.

Construct a residual plot of the fitted values versus residuals.

Select ALL features that indicate a good linear regression model:

```{webr}
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_A.csv")

model <- lm(___ ~ ___, data = quiz)

plot(___, ___,
     xlab = "Fitted Values",
     ylab = "Residuals")

abline(h = 0)
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_A.csv")

model <- lm(sales ~ ad_spend, data = quiz)

plot(model$fitted.values, model$residuals,
     xlab = "Fitted Values",
     ylab = "Residuals")

abline(h = 0)

# Random scatter around 0 
# No clear pattern
# Points spread out more as values increase
# Curved pattern

# correct answers: Random scatter around 0, and No clear pattern
```

## Q12 — Residual Plot {#q12b}

Using the `marketing_B.csv` dataset, fit a simple linear regression model with `sales` as the response and `ad_spend` as the predictor.

Construct a residual plot of the fitted values versus residuals.

Select ALL features that indicate a good linear regression model:

```{webr}
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_B.csv")

model <- lm(___ ~ ___, data = quiz)

plot(___, ___,
     xlab = "Fitted Values",
     ylab = "Residuals")

abline(h = 0)
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
quiz <- read.csv("marketing_B.csv")

model <- lm(sales ~ ad_spend, data = quiz)

plot(model$fitted.values, model$residuals,
     xlab = "Fitted Values",
     ylab = "Residuals")

abline(h = 0)

# Random scatter around 0
# No clear pattern
# Points spread out more as values increase
# Curved pattern

# correct answers: Random scatter around 0, and No clear pattern
```
